claims.claim_text: The module requires at least 8 GB RAM and strongly recommends 16 GB for the examples.
claims.anchor_text: **Prerequisites**: Module 1.1 complete, comfort using a terminal, basic Git workflow, and at least 8 GB RAM. A 16 GB machine is strongly recommended for the examples in this module.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Prerequisites**: Module 1.1 complete, comfort using a terminal, basic Git workflow, and enough free memory to run a small local model comfortably. More RAM will make the examples smoother.
claims.rationale: These exact hardware thresholds are environment-dependent and are not backed here by a stable primary source.

claims.claim_text: Marcus's monthly API bill crossed three hundred dollars.
claims.anchor_text: Marcus was not trying to become an AI infrastructure engineer when his monthly API bill crossed three hundred dollars.
claims.suggested_rewrite: One common reason developers explore local models is that repeated API-assisted coding experiments can quietly turn into a noticeable monthly bill.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed anecdote with a named person and precise cost detail, so the false specificity should be removed.

claims.claim_text: Quantization reduces memory use by storing model weights with fewer bits.
claims.rationale: The Hugging Face quantization guide explicitly states that lower-precision quantization reduces memory and computational costs.

claims.claim_text: 3B to 4B models typically fit 4 GB to 8 GB RAM and are fast but limited.
claims.anchor_text: | 3B to 4B             | 4 GB to 8 GB RAM     | Fast, limited depth  |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Small models         | Entry-level hardware | Fast, limited depth  |
claims.rationale: The exact RAM range is a contextual sizing estimate rather than a stable, source-backed fact.

claims.claim_text: 7B models typically fit 8 GB to 16 GB RAM and make good daily drivers.
claims.anchor_text: | 7B                   | 8 GB to 16 GB RAM    | Good daily driver    |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Mid-sized coding models | Common developer laptops | Good daily driver |
claims.rationale: This presents a precise memory band without a primary source and will drift across quantizations and runtimes.

claims.claim_text: 14B to 16B models typically fit 16 GB to 24 GB RAM and are better for refactoring.
claims.anchor_text: | 14B to 16B           | 16 GB to 24 GB RAM   | Better refactoring   |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Larger local coding models | Machines with more memory headroom | Better for harder refactors |
claims.rationale: The specific RAM guidance is an operational estimate, not a stable citation-backed number.

claims.claim_text: 22B to 32B models typically need 32 GB+ RAM or a GPU and are stronger but slower.
claims.anchor_text: | 22B to 32B           | 32 GB+ RAM or GPU    | Stronger, slower     |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Large local models   | High-memory workstations or GPUs | Stronger, but slower |
claims.rationale: The exact threshold is not stable across runtimes, quantizations, and hardware configurations.

claims.claim_text: Ollama is a local model runtime and manager that downloads models, runs an inference server, and exposes both a local API and CLI.
claims.rationale: The Ollama README documents the CLI, model execution flow, and REST API.

claims.claim_text: On macOS, Homebrew is the simplest Ollama installation path; Linux commonly uses the official install script and systemd; Windows exposes the same CLI through PowerShell.
claims.anchor_text: On macOS, Homebrew is the simplest installation path for many developers. On Linux, the official install script is common, and systemd may manage the service after installation. On Windows, the installer provides the same command-line interface through PowerShell.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Use the installation path recommended in the current Ollama documentation for your operating system, then verify the CLI with `ollama --version` before moving on.
claims.rationale: These platform-specific installation details are partly subjective and likely to drift as Ollama packaging changes.

claims.claim_text: Aider can work with local models in a Git repository, add files into context, edit them, show diffs, and commit the resulting changes.
claims.rationale: Aider's primary documentation directly describes file selection, editing, diffs, and git commits, but the host is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Continue supports Ollama-backed local models for editor workflows such as chat, inline editing, and autocomplete, using exact model names and an `apiBase` setting.
claims.span_hint: line 254 and config block at lines 256-278
claims.rationale: Continue's own Ollama guide covers chat/edit/autocomplete roles, exact model tags, and `apiBase`, but the docs host is off the allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Ollama exposes a local HTTP API by default, which lets editor tools talk to it.
claims.rationale: The Ollama README shows the default local REST API usage on `localhost:11434`.

further_reading.title: Ollama README
further_reading.why_relevant: Best starting point for the local runtime, installation paths, CLI usage, and the local HTTP API.

further_reading.title: Aider GitHub Repository
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful overview of terminal-based AI coding workflows and the project's Git-oriented usage model.

further_reading.title: Hugging Face Quantization Guide
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains why quantization changes memory and performance tradeoffs for local-model inference.

further_reading.title: Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Instruct Model Card
further_reading.why_relevant: Provides a concrete example of the model family, parameter count, and context window discussed in the module.

notes: Excluded code blocks, diagrams, quiz items, and hands-on exercise steps per pipeline rules. The Aider command examples in code blocks look time-sensitive relative to current docs, but code blocks are out of scope for citation injection, so I did not emit claim entries for them.
